policy_statement: Co-develop plans/projects with the disability community to design public space and the transportation system to advance disability justice (TLU.6-7).
mechanism_description: The city will co-develop public-space and transportation plans and projects with diverse disability community members so designs better reflect accessibility needs and strengthen equitable access and adaptive capacity.
primary_causal_pathway: Inclusive planning/design → ↑ accessible, equitable mobility & public-space usability → ↓ vulnerability/↑ adaptive capacity during climate hazards
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Human/system change: The city shifts planning and project delivery processes for streets, transit, and public spaces to be co-developed with disability community members, embedding accessibility and disability-justice set into designs and operations. (2) Mediating process: Improved physical and operational accessibility (e.g., barrier-free routes, safer crossings, clearer wayfinding, accessible curb ramps, sheltered/safer waiting areas) increases the ability of people with disabilities to move, reach services, and use public space reliably, including during service disruptions and emergencies. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced exposure and sensitivity to climate hazards and improved response/recovery capacity (adaptive capacity), especially during extreme heat, storms/flooding, wildfire smoke events, and evacuations.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced climate vulnerability (higher adaptive capacity and safer access to essential services) for people with disabilities through more accessible transport and public spaces.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: inclusive/co-developed transport and public-space planning → more accessible systems → improved ability to cope with and function during climate hazards → reduced vulnerability (adaptation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while accessibility can indirectly shift mode share (mitigation) or improve system efficiency (resource efficiency), the core and most consistent climate-relevant effect across all such policies is strengthening equitable access and adaptive capacity for climate disruptions.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a specific policy instance explicitly pairs co-design with emissions-reduction measures (e.g., transit electrification, VMT reduction), those would be secondary/parallel pathways, but not the dominant mechanism here.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy defined by disability-inclusive co-development to improve accessibility and equitable access inherently targets resilience and capacity to withstand disruptions, making Adaptation the correct primary category for the mechanism class.  
5) Note on subtypes: any secondary tagging (e.g., mitigation co-benefits from mode shift, safety, health) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on explicit evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could have mitigation co-benefits if redesigned public space and accessible transit materially increase transit/walking uptake and reduce car dependence, but that is not inherent or necessarily dominant in all instances of this mechanism.
key_indicators: “codeveloping plans and projects”; “with diverse elements of the disability community”; “understanding their needs before designs are complete”; “Design public space and the transportation system”
co_benefits: Improved everyday mobility access and equity; broader public-health and safety benefits in streets/transit; improved service quality and user satisfaction.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be construed as a regulatory/administrative mandate if embedded as a formal requirement for project approvals, but the phrasing is framed as a planning/design approach rather than an enforceable standard.
